thread.title: Iowa Supreme Court sides with Summit Carbon Solutions | Crime & Courts | kwwl.com
thread.title_full: Iowa Supreme Court sides with Summit Carbon Solutions | Crime & Courts | kwwl.com

author: David Warrington
title: Iowa Supreme Court sides with Summit Carbon Solutions | Crime & Courts | kwwl.com
text:
DES MOINES, Iowa (KWWL) -- The Iowa Supreme Court is siding with Summit Carbon Solutions. It upheld a district court decision, ruling against a Hardin County landowner who refused to allow pipeline surveyors on his property.
Attorney Brian Jorde said that he was not surprised by the ruling, but also explained it as a very limited ruling, and said further legal challenges will follow.
"This is really about what degree are we going to allow private, for profit speculators, that have a dream and an idea, to disrupt the lives and destroy the private property of Iowans."
Jorde, an attorney for the Hardin County landowner who refused to allow pipeline surveyors on his property, is giving his reaction.
The Iowa Supreme Court ruled in Summit's favor, saying the pipeline company met Iowa's code requirement for survey access, which includes enough prior notice and an informational meeting.
Jorde said, "No surprise, it was a very limited ruling. There's two ways to constitutionally challenge something. It's a facial challenge, which means no possible application of the law as written is applicable, and then there's one called as applied. We didn't bring an as applied in this case."
Jorde says he plans to though, and says that's part of their next legal challenges.
Earlier this week KWWL spoke with Lee Blank, CEO for Summit Carbon Solutions, who described misconceptions with the project.
Blank said, "Something that I hear quite often is that we're taking people's land, and that's just the farthest thing from the truth. It's a partnership with the landowner where we have a piece of our property that sits within a piece of their property, it's still their property."
In a statement released on Friday, Blank was appreciative of the court's decision, and said Summit will work collaboratively with landowners and treat them with respect.
The Iowa Utilities Board has approved the pipeline route in Iowa, including the right of eminent domain, which Jorde doesn't agree with.
Jorde explained, "Eminent domain is not appropriate for this project. At the end of the day, when we boil everything down, that's the key issue. Summit should treat landowners in Iowa like they have to in Minnesota. No eminent domain, they have to negotiate in good faith, you have to pay them fairly, and you have to have a contract that protects them."
Jorde said that an as applied constitutional challenge will be brought next, which he described as the key challenge.
The Summit pipeline took a step forward last week after [securing a permit](https://www.kwwl.com/news/national/summit-carbon-solutions-secures-north-dakota-pipeline-permit-advancing-co2-pipeline-project/article_8e5c210a-a373-11ef-9143-4fde1a9f7132.html) from the state of North Dakota.
